doom.driver: Israel and Iran exchange missile fire threatening Middle East truce. The exchange marked a major escalation in the already tense region. It came on the 100th day of a war that began on Feb. 28, further complicating efforts to end Middle East fighting.
doom.summary: Iran and Israel spent Sunday night lobbing missiles at each other — petrochemical complexes, West Bank settlements, Beirut suburbs, the works — before Iran announced an 'end of military operations,' which is a diplomatic way of saying 'we'll be back.' Meanwhile stock markets are tumbling, oil is climbing, and Zelensky's European allies are quietly listing peace conditions while Trump is busy rage-quitting NBC interviews. The region is holding together with the structural integrity of wet tissue paper.

factors.name: Geopolitics
factors.detail: Iran-Israel missile exchanges threaten a ceasefire that was already described as 'fragile,' with Lebanon now re-entering the picture via Hezbollah drone strikes.

factors.name: Governance
factors.detail: Trump storming out of a national security interview and installing an unqualified acting intelligence chief are not confidence-inspiring governance signals.

factors.name: AI Capability
factors.detail: DeepSeek V4 Pro reportedly beating GPT-5.5 Pro and Microsoft's AI chief announcing superintelligence is 'near' keep the capability race humming along.

factors.name: Wild Card
factors.detail: Ebola spreading at an unprecedented pace in DRC, while USAID — which would normally coordinate the response — has been substantially dismantled.

factors.name: Alignment
factors.detail: No major new alignment-specific developments today; the AI governance discourse remains background noise against louder kinetic crises.

doom.dalle_prompt: Pixel art, 16-bit SNES style, no text, no antialiasing — silhouette composition, dark crimson and charcoal palette, camera positioned at ground level inside a modest West Bank home looking outward through a single open doorway, the doorframe acting as a stark dark rectangle, a lone elderly Palestinian woman silhouetted in the doorway standing motionless with one hand pressed flat against the doorframe, her posture rigid and listening, the world outside her door rendered in deep burning amber and crimson — the pale ochre desert flatlands of the Jordan Valley stretching away toward the Dead Sea basin, the low silhouette of Jericho's palm trees and minaret just visible on the horizon, the dawn sky above slashed with multiple long white contrail arcs curving and crossing chaotically against the dark indigo-to-blood-orange gradient, the characteristic scratch-mark pattern of a mass intercept event glowing faintly overhead, the ground outside cracked pale tan desert soil, a single distant column of black smoke rising thin and vertical at the far left edge of the frame, firelight from somewhere unseen casting a faint orange glow along the base of the horizon, the interior behind the woman pitch black, her shadow stretching forward across the threshold into the dust, the entire scene saturated in oppressive dark crimson and charcoal with high contrast silhouette framing.

dawn.driver: New drug 'functionally cures' many hepatitis B virus infections.
dawn.blurb: HUMANITY CRACKS A VIRUS THAT KILLS 1.1 MILLION A YEAR
dawn.summary: Hepatitis B chronically infects roughly 250 million people worldwide and kills over a million annually — and until now, existing antivirals could suppress it but almost never eliminate it. A new drug has achieved what researchers call a 'functional cure' in many patients, clearing the infection in a way the field has chased for decades. On a day when Iran and Israel are trading missiles and markets are rattling, the quiet persistence of biomedical science delivering a genuine cure is exactly the kind of signal worth holding onto.

factors.name: Science
factors.detail: A functional cure for hepatitis B — long considered a near-impossible target — represents one of the most significant antiviral breakthroughs in years.

factors.name: Cooperation
factors.detail: Armenia's pro-Western government won a decisive election despite sustained Russian pressure, affirming democratic resilience in a contested region.

factors.name: Human Spirit
factors.detail: Hundreds of captives, including women and children, were freed from a Boko Haram mountain hideout, returning to families after months of captivity.

factors.name: Environment
factors.detail: New ship-alert camera systems are being deployed in San Francisco Bay to protect whales driven inshore by climate change, turning a vulnerability into a conservation intervention.

factors.name: Wild Card
factors.detail: Gut microbiome research is revealing that healthy bacteria may protect against severe sepsis, opening a new and accessible front in one of medicine's deadliest challenges.

dawn.dalle_prompt: Pixel art, 16-bit SNES style, no text, no antialiasing. Night scene, all light from artificial sources, deep shadows dominating. A cramped night-shift nurses' station in a Southeast Asian hospital corridor, darkness pressing in from all sides, the only illumination a single glowing desktop monitor casting rich gold and emerald light across a cluttered desk, the screen displaying a viral load graph with a dramatic plummeting line bottoming out to zero rendered in vibrant green pixels, a printed lab result sheet curling off the edge of the desk catching the monitor's amber glow, a small blister pack of antiviral pills pushed to the corner as if set aside for the last time, a half-drunk cup of tea steaming faintly in the cool darkness, the corridor beyond dissolving into deep shadow punctuated by a single distant exit sign glowing red, the monitor's emerald and gold light painting everything in saturated alive warmth against the surrounding dark, the scene radiating quiet awe — the numbers have changed, everything has changed, and no one is here yet to see it.
